analogy: Think of "The Fundamental Unit of Life" through the chapter's own opening images: Exploring Mixtures and their Separation Chapter 5 Have you ever wondered how sweet, white crystals of sugar are obtained from tall, green sugarcane plants? Or how do doctors detect diseases like malaria using just a few drops of blood? Many such everyday activities are made... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 5.1 How Can We Classify Mixtures?, 5.2 Solutions, 5.2.1 Concentration of a solution, 5.2.2 How do we express concentration?. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
The Fundamental Unit of Life is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that exploring Mixtures and their Separation Chapter 5 Have you ever wondered how sweet, white crystals of sugar are obtained from tall, green sugarcane plants?

The text explains that or how do doctors detect diseases like malaria using just a few drops of blood?

The text explains that many such everyday activities are made possible by techniques based on the fascinating science of separating mixtures.

The text explains that you have learnt about mixtures and some simple methods to separate them.

The text explains that in this chapter, you will explore mixtures in greater depth, including their properties, behaviour and the various techniques used to separate them.

The text explains that from industrial processes like sugar production to life-saving medical tests, the separation of mixtures plays a crucial role in our daily lives.

The text explains that grade 6 Curiosity Chapter 9 Think It Over y Why do suspended particles settle in muddy water over time but not in milk? y How is evaporation different from boiling? y Why do you see bright rays of sunlight when it passes through small gaps between the leaves of a dense tree?

The text explains that exploring Mixtures and their Separation 73 5.1 How Can We Classify Mixtures?

The text explains that you have learnt that a mixture of sugar and water has a uniform composition throughout.

The text explains that a well-stirred mixture of sugar and water is equally sweet in the first and the last sip.

The text explains that such a mixture is called a homogeneous mixture (Fig.

The text explains that other examples of homogeneous mixtures are vinegar (acetic acid in water), aerated drinks like soda (carbon dioxide in water), etc.

The text explains that on the other hand, a stirred mixture of sand and water is not uniform.

The text explains that the sand particles are easily visible in the water and settle with time.

The text explains that such a mixture is called a heterogeneous mixture (Fig.

The text explains that is the mixture of oil and water homogeneous or heterogeneous?

The text explains that direct the light from a laser pointer through the beakers containing the mixtures (Fig.

The text explains that 5.3) and observe it from the side of the beaker in a direction perpendicular to the laser beam.

The text explains that safety first: Do not look directly into the laser beam.

The text explains that 5.2: Heterogeneous mixture Grade 8 Curiosity Chapter 9 (a) (b) (c) Fig.

The text explains that when farmers spray pesticides on their crops, they must mix the right amount of pesticide with a fixed amount of water to prepare a solution.

The text explains that the right proportion is always essential when preparing a solution.

The text explains that grade 8 Curiosity Chapter 9 Grade 6 Curiosity Chapter 6 Note Not all sugary drinks prepared at home or sold in the market are ORS.

The text explains that pause and Ponder 5.2.3 Solubility of substances You have learnt that the maximum amount of solute that dissolves in a fixed quantity of the solvent (100 mL or 100 g) is called its solubility at a given temperature.

The text explains that a solution that cannot dissolve any more solute at that temperature is called a saturated solution.

The text explains that this is because the solubility of a solid solute in a liquid solvent generally increases with temperature.

The text explains that on the other hand, in the case of gases dissolved in liquids, their solubility generally decreases with an increase in temperature.

The text explains that grade 8 Curiosity Chapter 9 Threads of Curiosity A saline drip in hospitals is usually 0.9 % m/v sodium chloride (common salt) in water.

The text explains that a graph of solubility versus temperature is called a solubility curve.

The text explains that the x-axis shows temperature (°C), whereas the y-axis indicates the solubility of the solute in grams per 100 g of water.

The text explains that 5.6: Solubility curves of compounds ‘A’ and ‘B’ in water 0 400 y x 350 300 250 241 200 100 150 50 10 20 30 40 50 60 70 80 Temperature (°C) Solubility (g per 100 g water) Compound ‘A’ Compound ‘B’ 287 Based on the information from the above graph, predict which of the two compounds, ‘A’ or ‘B’, will dissolve more in a given amount of water at a given temperature?

The text explains that (iii) The solubility of _____________ increases more than that of ______________ with an increase in the temperature.

The text explains that what do you think will happen if you make a saturated solution at a higher temperature and cool it slowly?

The text explains that exploration|Grade 9 78 You might have observed many types of crystals in everyday life, such as rock salt crystals (Fig.

The text explains that in laboratories, the process of forming crystals from a saturated solution is called crystallization.

The text explains that the principle of purification by crystallization is based on the differences in the solubility of a substance at different temperatures.

The text explains that 5.8: Steps involved in the process of crystallization Glass rod Glass rod Burner Water bath Conical funnel Watch glass Laboratory stand Copper sulfate crystals Tripod stand Beaker Beaker Filtrate Beaker Copper sulfate solution (a) (b) (c) Fig.

The text explains that 5.7: Crystals of (a) rock salt, (b) candy sugar, and (c) frost Watch glass

The text explains that if a hot, saturated solution of copper sulfate is cooled rapidly in ice-cold water, smaller and less well-formed crystals will form than if it is cooled slowly at room temperature.

The text explains that pause and Ponder Activity 5.4: Let us describe a process Observe Fig.

The text explains that can you describe the process in your own words?

The text explains that 5.9: Steps followed in the salt manufacturing process Ready to Go Beyond (a) (b) Have you visited a place where large crystal deposits can be observed in nature?

The text explains that such crystals can be found in various locations, including mines, caves and even within the Earth’s crust.

The text explains that during the process, vapours of the lower-boiling liquid are passed through a condenser where they are cooled, usually by circulating water or air and condensed as a pure liquid.

The text explains that 5.11: Distillation process India’s Scientific Contributions Crystallization of salt was an ancient process used by the local communities of the coastal areas in India.

The text explains that the process of distillation has been used for a long time for various purposes, including extraction of fragrances from flowers to make perfumes.

The text explains that you must have noticed the pleasant earthy smell that rises from the ground after the first rain.

The text explains that in Kannauj, a town in Uttar Pradesh known as the perfume capital of India, this beautiful fragrance is captured and turned into a natural perfume called Mitti ka Ittar (earthy fragrance).

The text explains that (i) Identify the separation technique marked as ‘S’.

The text explains that (iii) Which of the following mixtures can be separated by the technique identified above?

The text explains that mixtures: (a) water — acetone (b) water — salt (c) acetone — alcohol (d) sand — salt (e) alcohol — chloroform (f) alcohol — benzene S ...........................................

The text explains that 5.26 A B C y Demonstrate the Tyndall effect using different colloids.

Source: NCERT Class 9 Science, Chapter 5: The Fundamental Unit of Life

concise_notes.term: 5.3 Methods of Separation of Homogeneous Mixtures
concise_notes.definition: You have learnt about mixtures and some simple methods to separate them.
5.3 Methods of Separation of Homogeneous Mixtures 5.3.1 Crystallization You have learnt to prepare a saturated solution.
5.3.2 Distillation A homogeneous mixture of two miscible liquids can be separated by heating the mixture until the liquid with the lower boiling point vaporises.
Exploration|Grade 9 82 5.3.3 Paper Chromatography Have you ever observed what happens when a drop of water falls on something written with a sketch pen on a paper?

prelims_traps: Do not treat 5.1 How Can We Classify Mixtures? and 5.2 Solutions as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to The Fundamental Unit of Life; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain The Fundamental Unit of Life through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 5.1 How Can We Classify Mixtures? and 5.2 Solutions to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
